test_rationale: This scenario targets the underspecified semantics of delete_name at the zone apex. The initial zone contains only the required apex SOA/NS plus one unrelated helper A RR below the apex so the update’s effect is isolated. A single unauthenticated UPDATE deleting the apex name forces the server to choose between policy rejection and some form of apex state mutation, and follow-up SOA/NS queries make the resulting state observable. That creates a Ferret-executable binary oracle across BIND and Knot on both reply code and post-state.

group_1.behavior: Reject the apex delete_name as policy-forbidden or semantic failure, preserving the existing apex SOA and NS RRsets.
group_1.post_state: apex SOA and NS remain unchanged

group_2.behavior: Apply a different apex-delete policy, such as accepting the UPDATE or handling it as a broader apex purge/minimal-retention transition.
group_2.post_state: apex SOA/NS state differs from the original zone, potentially leaving only mandatory apex data or a modified apex RRset set

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §3.4.3 defines delete_name generically but does not special-case the zone apex, while RFC 3007 §3.1.1 only says SOA and NS SHOULD NOT be modified by normal users. RFC 2181 does not mandate a single processor transition for apex deletion either. As a result, implementations may reject the request with a policy RCODE or accept it and mutate the apex RRsets differently, making both the UPDATE reply and the post-update apex state underspecified.
